failure_modes.text: Commitment can hide what becomes impossible once resources, time, or attention are locked into one path.
failure_modes.mitigation: Before committing, explicitly list what becomes impossible: name the specific projects, options, or capabilities that this commitment forecloses.

failure_modes.text: Decision paralysis can keep the team comparing endless alternatives instead of acting on a reversible test.
failure_modes.mitigation: Set a decision timer for reversible choices: if the decision is easily reversible, cap comparison time and run a small experiment instead of endlessly modeling alternatives.

failure_modes.text: A request can look cheap in isolation while still stealing the same launch window, engineering time, or management attention from the highest-value move.
failure_modes.source_quote: Cheap add-on displaces the real priority
failure_modes.mitigation: Price requests in the same currency as the best alternative: express the cost of this commitment in terms of what the same resources would deliver on the highest-value alternative.

premortem_questions.text: Before approval: name the single most valuable thing this decision makes impossible or significantly harder for the next 12 months.
premortem_questions.source_quote: Before approving: name the single most valuable thing this decision makes impossible or significantly harder for the next 12 months.

premortem_questions.text: Which comparison in this plan is pretending to be irreversible even though we could run it as a bounded test first, and what learning are we giving up by waiting for certainty before acting?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Which comparison in this plan is pretending to be irreversible even though we could run it as a bounded test first, and what learning are we giving up by waiting for certainty before acting?

premortem_questions.text: Which initiative in this plan looks inexpensive only because its cost is being charged to the same people, budget, or launch window needed by a more valuable move?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Which initiative in this plan looks inexpensive only because its cost is being charged to the same people, budget, or launch window needed by a more valuable move, and what exactly are we delaying by saying yes?

heuristics.text: Upgrade simple pro-con thinking into a comparison that includes the implicit costs of the best foregone option.
Use opportunity cost as a prioritization filter so scarce time and attention flow toward the highest-leverage work.
Force every recommendation to answer compared to what before calling it attractive.

curation_notes.summary: The chunk set stays close to the raw displacement logic and avoids over-expanding into generic personal trade-off advice.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor expansions into broad personal-life examples because the strongest raw substrate is resource-allocation discipline.
open_questions: The heuristics are normalized from the raw playbook, but each remains tightly grounded in explicit comparison and prioritization language.

allies: The raw ally section is strong on prioritization, first principles, and scenario analysis for later relation preservation.
antagonists: The raw antagonist section explicitly supports sunk-cost-fallacy, confirmation-bias, and availability-style conflicts.
structured_tensions: The raw source explicitly preserves tensions against status-quo-bias, sunk-cost-fallacy, and anchoring.
